Subsections.Title: Overview
Subsections.Content:
This Operation and Maintenance (O&M) Manual provides information and instructions on how to operate and maintain the United States Air Force Distributed Ground Station (AF DCGS) Advanced Processing System Plus (APS+) software.
The intended audience for this manual are U-2S Dragonlady aircraft / Advanced Synthetic Aperture Radar System-2A (ASARS-2A) high-resolution, multimode, long-range, air-to-ground radar sensor Mission Planners (MPs) and Direct Mission Support Maintenance Personnel (DMSMP) using APS+ installed in AF DCGS equipment located at operational Distributed Ground Sites (DGS) and Raytheon ASARS-2A engineers supporting in-plant maintenance and sustainment activities. This manual assumes the user already has working knowledge of the ASARS-2A sensor, server, workstation operating systems and graphical user interfaces (GUIs), and related computer networking. It is not meant to serve as a complete reference for all mission activities supported by MPs and DMSMP.
The APS+ software modernizes the legacy APS software used operationally for over 20 years. This modernization was accomplished to align with AF DCGS technology roadmaps and to make the APS+ software more technically compatible, functionally robust, and easier to maintain.
APS+ displays ASARS-2A sensor health, status, and image quality and is used for U-2S / ASARS-2A mission planning.
APS+ software is installed in the Distributed Common Ground Station (DCGS) System Integration Lab (DSIL), East Coast Hub (ECH), and West Coast Hub (WCH). APS+ is capable of supporting multiple instances, allowing APS+ to be remotely accessible by the deployed DGS sites.
The APS+ software is capable of being customized at each operating site to accommodate differences in configuration, interfaces, or mission needs. Workstations in each AF DCGS may provide slightly different shortcuts to launch certain APS+ applications. This manual does not cover site-specific APS+ customizations.

Subsections.Title: Scope
Subsections.Content:
Type of manual: Operation and Maintenance Manual
End item nomenclature: United States Air Force (AF) Distributed Common Ground Station (DCGS) Advanced Processing System Plus (APS+) Software
End item purpose: Display ASARS-2A sensor health, status, and image quality and provide U-2S aircraft / ASARS-2A sensor mission planning for Intelligence, Reconnaissance, and Surveillance (ISR) mission operations.
Manual Use: To provide AF DCGS APS+ software operation and maintenance information and instructions. The information and instructions include APS+ general information, operator instructions, maintenance instructions, and supporting information.
Figures and Tables: Figures and tables are numbered and titled. The figures and tables are sequentially numbered within the subsection.

Subsections.Title: System Description
Subsections.Content:
At established AF DCGS sites and Distributed Ground Station (DGS) deployed locations, operators plan navigational routes for the U-2S aircraft to follow during the mission. Using the planned navigational routes, operators can then perform mission planning by identifying potential targets for imagery collection planning and scene planning to capture a target’s imagery using one of the ASARS-2A modes.
A completed mission plan is uplinked to the U-2S aircraft / ASARS-2A sensor airborne system for collection and processing. As the aircraft approaches a preplanned scene, raw data is collected as Video Phase History (VPH) data by the Receiver Exciter Controller (REC), and then transmitted to the On-Board Processor (OBP) for recording and/or processing.
Once the data is received by the OBP, data is captured and processed into imagery. Users have the option of recording imagery or raw VPH data onto the Sensor Storage Array (SSA). Any imagery which is created is dispatched to an AF DCGS, where analysts may evaluate it.
At any point during the mission, operators may edit the mission plan by planning additional scenes or altering parameters of previously planned scenes. Updated scenes may then be uplinked to re-task the ASARS-2A sensor. Scenes requiring immediate collection may also be uplinked to the ASARS-2A sensor for ad hoc tactical requirements.

Subsections.Title: Operating Sites
Subsections.Content:
Each operating site is designated as a core site, reach site, or test site. Core sites and reach sites are mission sites, while test sites function like core sites but do not support operational missions. Reach sites have a line-of-site (LOS) link to the sensor and transmit imagery and telemetry to core sites via satellite. Core sites have the capability to process multiple concurrent missions independently.
Functions performed by the APS at core sites include mission planning, mission monitoring, sensor control, screening, dissemination, and exploitation. The following shows a list of sites and their site designation.
Table  SEQ Table \* ARABIC 3 Operating Sites

Subsections.Title: Configurations
Subsections.Content:
There are three basic ground datalink configurations for receiving ASARS-2A mission data and transmitting commands and mission plan updates.
Direct Line of Sight (LOS) configuration
Reach configuration
Beyond Line of Sight (BLOS) configuration

Subsections.Title: Direct LOS Configuration
Subsections.Content:
In the Direct LOS configuration (Figure 1), the airborne system downlinks data directly to an AF DCGS site equipped with a compatible Common Data Link (CDL) receiving antenna. The AF DCGS can also uplink commands via CDL to the airborne system.
In a Direct LOS configuration, the AF DCGS site typically performs all of the sensor maintenance, sensor re-tasking, imagery screening, and ASARS-2A sensor imagery exploitation. The Flight Test Facility (FTF) and DGS-3 sites typically use a Direct LOS configuration.
Figure  SEQ Figure \* ARABIC 1 Direct LOS Configuration

Subsections.Title: Reach Configuration
Subsections.Content:
In the Reach configuration (Figure 2), the airborne system downlinks data via CDL to a ground relay site located within the footprint of the airborne CDL antenna. The ground relay site then uses a satellite communications (SATCOM) link to relay the data to an AF DCGS site. ASARS-2A sensor commands can also be uplinked via the reverse path: from the AF DCGS site to the ground relay site via SATCOM link, then to the airborne sensor via CDL. The connection between a ground relay site and a particular AF DCGS site is point-to-point. A ground relay site can only connect to one AF DCGS site at a time.
In a Reach configuration, primary sensor control and monitoring is usually performed at the AF DCGS site. However, the ground relay site retains a capability to assert ASARS-2A sensor control if necessary. The Forward Operating Location (FOL) site uses a Reach configuration. Most AF DCGS sites are capable of using FOL as a reach site if dictated by mission needs.
Figure  SEQ Figure \* ARABIC 2 Reach Configuration

Subsections.Title: BLOS Configuration
Subsections.Content:
In the BLOS configuration (Figure 3), the airborne system downlinks data and receives commands via another communications system on the U-2S aircraft which uses a different SATCOM architecture than a reach site. The receiving ground site for a BLOS SATCOM link is called a Relay Ground Station (RGS); there are currently two RGS sites (RGS-1 and RGS-2). Once received by an RGS site, the data is made available to one or more DGS sites via ground communication links. AF DCGS coordinates which DGS site is the lead for the mission (“prime”) and which other DGS sites will participate as federated mission partners. Although data may be disseminated from an RGS to multiple DGS sites, only one DGS site at a time is allowed to uplink commands to the sensor via the RGS.
In a BLOS configuration, primary sensor control is performed at a single DGS site; all sites receive sensor data and telemetry. Any DGS site is capable of supporting BLOS operations.
Figure  SEQ Figure \* ARABIC 3 BLOS Configuration

Subsections.Title: Multiple Datalink Capabilities
Subsections.Content:
The U-2S aircraft has multiple datalink capabilities (Figure 4). The U-2S aircraft can use two datalinks at the same time; either one CDL datalink and one BLOS SATCOM datalink or two different, independent, concurrent CDL datalinks.
If more than one datalink is active, the ASARS-2A sensor can only accept commands from one datalink. By default, the sensor will accept commands from the pilot selected primary datalink. A Control Data Unit (CDU) Reset command can be sent to instruct ASARS-2A sensor to accept commands from a specific datalink, regardless of the pilot selected primary datalink.
Figure  SEQ Figure \* ARABIC 4 Multiple Datalink Capabilities

Subsections.Title: Mission Threads
Subsections.Content: Dual Mission refers to two U-2S aircraft simultaneously flying separate missions. An AF DCGS site is capable of supporting Dual Mission simultaneous mission threads. Each mission thread receives data only from the designated source of mission thread. To be updated…

Subsections.Title: ASARS-2A Data from Reach Site to Core Site
Subsections.Content:
This scenario occurs only if an AF DCGS core site has a Direct LOS link to the ASARS-2A sensor (Figure 5). The AF DCGS site VIP-C receives ASARS-2A sensor files and sends to the image server. Processes on the image server connect to the VIP-C to receive telemetry and VIP-C status. Uplink commands are passed from the image server through the VIP-C to the ASARS-2A sensor. To be updated…
Figure  SEQ Figure \* ARABIC 5 ASARS-2A Data from Reach Site to Core Site (To be updated…)

Subsections.Title: ASARS-2A Data from RGS to Core Site
Subsections.Content:
This scenario occurs if an AF DCGS core site receives and displays imagery, MTI data and telemetry transmitted from the reach site (Figure 6). The AF DCGS core site sends uplink commands to the reach site. To be updated…
Figure  SEQ Figure \* ARABIC 6 ASARS-2A from RGS to Core Site (To be updated…)

Subsections.Title: APS+ Description
Subsections.Content: The APS+ software provides ASARS-2A sensor health and status monitoring, sensor collection planning, sensor command and control, and sensor imagery screening capabilities. APS+ software is installed on Open Architecture Distributed Common Ground Station (OA DCGS) system, integrated with enterprise services and mission capabilities at central hub sites and is remotely accessed by operational users at individual DGS sites.

Subsections.Title: APS+ Software Environment
Subsections.Content:
APS+ is a software component which will be deployed as part of the OA DCGS subsystem within the AF DCGS enterprise. APS+ belongs to the imagery intelligence (IMINT) segment. APS+ operates within a virtual machine (VM) hosted on the OA DCGS hardware stack at operational hub sites. APS+ user-facing applications are made available to users at Distributed Ground Station (DGS) sites which are logically and often physically separated from the hub locations.
Similar to other OA DCGS components, APS+ interfaces with standard enterprise services which provide functions such as identity management, access control, auditing, intrusion detection, time of day, hardware and software health and status monitoring, asset management, and other capabilities. Interfaces to standard enterprise services are primarily related to the management of the AF DCGS enterprise, not specifically related to functional interfaces required for mission execution.

Subsections.Title: APS+ Interfaces
Subsections.Content:
The APS+ internal and external interfaces both have input and output functions (Figure 7). APS+ internal interfaces are defined as APS+ software in one context interfacing to APS+ software in another context. APS+ external interfaces are defined as APS+ software interfacing to anything which is not APS+ software. Additional details to be provided…
Figure  SEQ Figure \* ARABIC 7 APS+ External Interfaces (To be updated…)

Subsections.Title: Mission Control Interface (MCI)
Subsections.Content: The APS+ software Mission Configuration Interface (MCI) application is used to define a mission at a Core or Reach Site. Prior to takeoff, the mission must be defined using the MCI application. Once the mission is defined and started, background processes are launched to provide interfaces for the VIP-C, telemetry, uplink, and other functions, as appropriate.

Subsections.Title: Sensor Control Workstation (SCWS)
Subsections.Content: The APS+ software Sensor Control Workstation (SCWS) application connects a DGS to the airborne system via datalink. The SCWS application displays information sent from the airborne system to the DGS and provides an interface to uplink commands from the DGS to the airborne system. Prior to takeoff, the mission must be defined and started using the MCI application, then the DGS to airborne system datalink must be established using the SCWS application.

Subsections.Title: Common Sensor Planner (CSP)
Subsections.Content:
The APS+ software Common Sensor Planner (CSP) application is the interface to load the mission plan into the ASARS-2A sensor. During a mission, the CSP application can function as a Controlling CSP or as a non- controlling Monitoring CSP.
The Controlling CSP function is used exclusively by the MP during pre-mission planning and during the execution of the mission. During the mission, it is used to modify the mission plan, uplink the mission plan to the sensor, update the database for completion of scenes, and output scene status and mission plan information to the External Tasking Service (ETS). Only one instance of the Controlling CSP function is run for a mission thread during a mission.
The Monitoring CSP function is used by DGS personnel other than the MP during a mission to maintain situational awareness of the mission. The Monitoring CSP function does not output data to databases and external recipients. Multiple instances of the Monitoring CSP function can operate simultaneously.

Title: OPERATOR INSTRUCTIONS
Content: At DGS sites, operators plan navigational routes which the U-2 aircraft will follow. From these routes, operators may plan and identify potential targets for imagery, and plan a scene to capture the imagery of that target using one of the ASARS-2A modes.

Subsections.Title: MCI Operator Instructions
Subsections.Content: This subsection provides MCI displays and controls information, MCI startup and shutdown procedures, MCI functional procedures, and mission thread operation procedures.

Subsections.Title: MCI Core Site Options
Subsections.Content:
Figure  SEQ Figure \* ARABIC 16 MCI Operation - Core Site
Exploitation Segment Options. The Exploitation Segment check boxes specify where to send data. In most cases, "data" refers to imagery files. However, in a few cases, “data” also mean mission planning data, telemetry data, and/or status files.
The following are the Exploitation Segment check box options on the "Start Mission" window. The description of each option details the type of data that is sent.
Note
This list in the GUI is configurable per site. Each site may have a different list of check box options displayed in this GUI.
Table  SEQ Table \* ARABIC 4 Exploitation Segment Options (To be updated…)

Data: sends imagery mosaics  to the ingest directory for TIID application,  which then fills in the tactical image ID in the NITF header and forwards the NITF file to the TMAN
sends MTI NITF files  to MTI/TS via a TCP/IP socket connection
deposits imagery directly into a directory on the TMAN for passing to the high-side security enclave
GEOINT Data Transformation Service/Airborne Communication Architecture
sends imagery mosaics  to GDTS/ACA-R using SFTP
sends imagery mosaics  to the IAS system using RDTP
sends NITF tiles to Site 6 to support Change Detection, only intended for ASARS-2A Reach (LOS) mission
sends NITF tiles to Site 10 to support Change Detection, only intended for ASARS-2A Reach (LOS) missions
sends imagery, telemetry, and  mission plan information  to DGETS for visualization on Google Earth
SFTP mission plan information  to other DGSes to support federation
send imagery  to DGS-86, Environment A for supporting imagery federation using RDTP
send imagery  to DGS-86, Environment B for supporting imagery federation  using RDTP
